# TSRX - AI/LLM Documentation

## Overview

TSRX (TypeScript Render Extensions) is a **TypeScript language extension** for
building declarative UIs. It compiles one `.tsrx` source file to **React**,
**Preact**, **Solid**, **Vue**, or **Ripple**. TSRX is the
source language; the target picks the runtime semantics.

Think of TSRX as a spiritual successor to JSX: the same mental model of
embedding UI directly in TypeScript, but with its own flavor. Control flow,
scoped styles, and locals sit in the template as first-class syntax instead
of being squeezed through expression slots, and the language stays aware of
them all the way through to the compiled output.

**Key characteristics:**

- **One source, many targets**: `@tsrx/react`, `@tsrx/preact`, `@tsrx/solid`, `@tsrx/vue`, `@tsrx/ripple`
- **Statement-based JSX**: JSX elements are statements, not expressions
- **Declarative control flow**: `if`, `for`, `switch`, `try` inside templates
- **Lexical template scoping**: declare locals next to the JSX that uses them
- **Lazy prop destructuring**: `&{ ... }` and `&[ ... ]` preserve reactivity
- **Scoped styles**: per-component `<style>` blocks with automatic class hashing
- **TypeScript-first**: `.tsrx` is a superset of TypeScript; types flow through

**Canonical compiler packages**: `@tsrx/core` (framework-agnostic
parser/analyzer), `@tsrx/react` (React codegen), `@tsrx/preact` (Preact codegen),
`@tsrx/solid` (Solid codegen), `@tsrx/vue` (Vue/Vapor codegen), `@tsrx/ripple`
(Ripple codegen).

## Installation & Targets

```bash
# Pick one target plus a bundler integration

# React target
npm install @tsrx/react
npm install -D @tsrx/vite-plugin-react     # Vite
npm install -D @tsrx/rspack-plugin-react   # Rspack
npm install -D @tsrx/turbopack-plugin-react # Next.js / Turbopack
npm install -D @tsrx/bun-plugin-react      # Bun

# Preact target
npm install @tsrx/preact
npm install -D @tsrx/vite-plugin-preact    # Vite
npm install -D @tsrx/rspack-plugin-preact  # Rspack
npm install -D @tsrx/bun-plugin-preact     # Bun

# Solid target
npm install @tsrx/solid solid-js @solidjs/web
npm install -D @tsrx/vite-plugin-solid
npm install -D @tsrx/rspack-plugin-solid
npm install -D @tsrx/bun-plugin-solid      # Bun

# Vue target
npm install @tsrx/vue vue vue-jsx-vapor
npm install -D @tsrx/vite-plugin-vue
npm install -D @tsrx/rspack-plugin-vue
npm install -D @tsrx/bun-plugin-vue        # Bun

# Ripple target
npm install ripple @ripple-ts/vite-plugin
```

Vue's Vite setup should use `tsrxVue()` from `@tsrx/vite-plugin-vue`,
and editor/typecheck configs should set `jsxImportSource: 'vue-jsx-vapor'`.

### Rspack setup (React target)

`@tsrx/rspack-plugin-react` is an Rspack plugin class that registers two
module rules: one compiles `.tsrx` → TSX via `@tsrx/react` and chains
`builtin:swc-loader` for the final JSX transform, and one routes the sibling
`?tsrx-css&lang.css` import emitted by the JS loader through Rspack's
built-in CSS module type (`type: 'css/auto'`).

```js
// rspack.config.js
import { TsrxReactRspackPlugin } from '@tsrx/rspack-plugin-react';

export default {
  plugins: [new TsrxReactRspackPlugin()],
};
```

Options: `{ jsxImportSource?: string }` — overrides the JSX import source
(defaults to `'react'`). The plugin also pushes `.tsrx` into
`resolve.extensions` and enables `experiments.css` when unset.

### Rspack setup (Preact target)

`@tsrx/rspack-plugin-preact` is an Rspack plugin class that registers two
module rules: one compiles `.tsrx` → TSX via `@tsrx/preact` and chains
`builtin:swc-loader` for the final JSX transform, and one routes the sibling
`?tsrx-css&lang.css` import emitted by the JS loader through Rspack's
built-in CSS module type (`type: 'css/auto'`).

```js
// rspack.config.js
import { TsrxPreactRspackPlugin } from '@tsrx/rspack-plugin-preact';

export default {
  plugins: [new TsrxPreactRspackPlugin()],
};
```

Options: `{ jsxImportSource?: string, suspenseSource?: string }` — override the
JSX import source (defaults to `'preact'`) and, if needed, the module used for
`Suspense` (defaults to `'preact/compat'`). The plugin also pushes `.tsrx`
into `resolve.extensions` and enables `experiments.css` when unset.

### Rspack setup (Solid target)

`@tsrx/rspack-plugin-solid` is an Rspack plugin class that registers two
module rules: one compiles `.tsrx` → TSX via `@tsrx/solid`, then runs the
final TypeScript + Solid JSX transform through `babel-loader` with
`@babel/preset-typescript` and `babel-preset-solid`; the other routes the
sibling `?tsrx-css&lang.css` import emitted by the JS loader through Rspack's
built-in CSS module type (`type: 'css/auto'`).

```js
// rspack.config.js
import { TsrxSolidRspackPlugin } from '@tsrx/rspack-plugin-solid';

export default {
  plugins: [new TsrxSolidRspackPlugin()],
};
```

Options: `{ hot?: boolean }` — controls whether the plugin adds
`solid-refresh/babel` to the Babel pipeline. It defaults to `true` unless
Rspack is running in production mode. The plugin also pushes `.tsrx` into
`resolve.extensions` and enables `experiments.css` when unset.

### Rspack setup (Vue target)

`@tsrx/rspack-plugin-vue` is an Rspack plugin class that registers two module
rules: one compiles `.tsrx` → TSX via `@tsrx/vue`, runs the result through
`vue-jsx-vapor`, and strips the remaining TypeScript syntax with
`builtin:swc-loader`; the other routes the sibling `?tsrx-css&lang.css`
import emitted by the JS loader through Rspack's built-in CSS module type
(`type: 'css/auto'`).

```js
// rspack.config.js
import { TsrxVueRspackPlugin } from '@tsrx/rspack-plugin-vue';

export default {
  plugins: [new TsrxVueRspackPlugin()],
};
```

Options: `{ vapor?: { macros?: boolean | object, compiler?: { runtimeModuleName?: string } } }`
— forwards options to `vue-jsx-vapor`. By default the plugin enables macros
and uses `runtimeModuleName: 'vue-jsx-vapor'`. Like the other Rspack plugins,
it pushes `.tsrx` into `resolve.extensions` and enables `experiments.css` when
unset.

### Turbopack setup (React target)

`@tsrx/turbopack-plugin-react` exports a Next.js config helper that installs a
pair of Turbopack rules for `.tsrx` files. The JS rule compiles `.tsrx` → TSX
via `@tsrx/react` and returns the result to Turbopack as `*.tsx` so Next's
React pipeline can handle the final JSX transform. When a component contains a
local `<style>` block, the loader injects a sibling `?tsrx-css&lang.css`
import, and the second Turbopack rule re-runs the compiler to extract the
scoped CSS and hands it back to Turbopack as a CSS module type.

```ts
// next.config.ts
import tsrxReactTurbopack from '@tsrx/turbopack-plugin-react';

export default tsrxReactTurbopack({
  reactStrictMode: true,
});
```

### Bun setup (React target)

`@tsrx/bun-plugin-react` exports a Bun plugin that compiles `.tsrx` → TSX via
`@tsrx/react`, runs the final automatic JSX transform through Bun's TSX
pipeline, and serves component-local `<style>` blocks as sibling virtual CSS
modules.

```ts
// build.ts
import tsrxReact from '@tsrx/bun-plugin-react';

await Bun.build({
  entrypoints: ['./src/App.tsrx'],
  outdir: './dist',
  target: 'browser',
  plugins: [tsrxReact()],
});
```

Options: `{ jsxImportSource?: string, emitCss?: boolean, include?: RegExp,
exclude?: RegExp | RegExp[] }` — override the automatic JSX runtime import
source (defaults to `'react'`), disable virtual CSS emission, or filter which
files the plugin compiles.

### Bun setup (Preact target)

`@tsrx/bun-plugin-preact` follows the same Bun build/test integration shape:
it compiles `.tsrx` → TSX via `@tsrx/preact`, runs Bun's TSX transform for
Preact's automatic JSX runtime, and serves component-local `<style>` blocks as
sibling virtual CSS modules.

```ts
// build.ts
import tsrxPreact from '@tsrx/bun-plugin-preact';

await Bun.build({
  entrypoints: ['./src/App.tsrx'],
  outdir: './dist',
  target: 'browser',
  plugins: [tsrxPreact()],
});
```

Options: `{ jsxImportSource?: string, suspenseSource?: string, emitCss?: boolean,
include?: RegExp, exclude?: RegExp | RegExp[] }` — override the automatic JSX
runtime import source (defaults to `'preact'`), override the module used for
`Suspense` (defaults to `'preact/compat'`), disable virtual CSS emission, or
filter which files the plugin compiles.

### Bun setup (Solid target)

`@tsrx/bun-plugin-solid` compiles `.tsrx` → TSX via `@tsrx/solid`, runs the
final TypeScript + Solid JSX transform through Babel with
`@babel/preset-typescript` and `babel-preset-solid`, and serves
component-local `<style>` blocks as sibling virtual CSS modules.

```ts
// build.ts
import tsrxSolid from '@tsrx/bun-plugin-solid';

await Bun.build({
  entrypoints: ['./src/App.tsrx'],
  outdir: './dist',
  target: 'browser',
  plugins: [tsrxSolid()],
});
```

Options: `{ solid?: object, emitCss?: boolean, include?: RegExp, exclude?: RegExp | RegExp[] }`
forwards options to `babel-preset-solid`, disables virtual CSS emission when
requested, or filters which files the plugin compiles.

### Bun setup (Vue target)

`@tsrx/bun-plugin-vue` compiles `.tsrx` → TSX via `@tsrx/vue`, runs the
downstream `vue-jsx-vapor` transform, strips the remaining TypeScript syntax
with Bun, and serves component-local `<style>` blocks as sibling virtual CSS
modules.

```ts
// build.ts
import tsrxVue from '@tsrx/bun-plugin-vue';

await Bun.build({
  entrypoints: ['./src/App.tsrx'],
  outdir: './dist',
  target: 'browser',
  plugins: [tsrxVue()],
});
```

Options: `{ vapor?: { macros?: boolean | object, compiler?: { runtimeModuleName?: string } }, emitCss?: boolean, include?: RegExp, exclude?: RegExp | RegExp[] }`
— forwards options to `vue-jsx-vapor`, disables virtual CSS emission when
requested, or filters which files the plugin compiles. Like the Vite and Rspack
Vue integrations, editor typechecking should set
`jsxImportSource: 'vue-jsx-vapor'`.

Files use the `.tsrx` extension. The compiler transforms the same source
differently per target — e.g. `if` lowers to `<Show>` on Solid, an IIFE +
conditional return on React/Preact, a `defineVaporComponent(...)` body on Vue,
and a reactive block on Ripple.

## Core Syntax

### Components

Components use the `component` keyword, not `function`. Props are a single
typed parameter. The template lives directly in the body — no `return`.

```tsrx
export component Button({ label, onClick }: {
  label: string;
  onClick: () => void;
}) {
  <button class="btn" {onClick}>
    {label}
  </button>

  <style>
    .btn {
      padding: 0.5rem 1rem;
      border-radius: 4px;
    }
  </style>
}
```

**LLM DO:**
- Use `component Name(props: Props) { ... }` for any UI building block.
- Use `export component` for public components.
- Keep the template, event handlers, and `<style>` in the component body.

**LLM DON'T:**
- Do not `return <JSX />` from a component body.
- Do not assign `.tsrx` UI to a variable outside a `<tsrx>...</tsrx>`, `<>...</>`, or `<tsx>...</tsx>` wrapper.
- Do not mix `function Component()` and `component Component()` patterns.

### Statement-based JSX

JSX elements are **statements**, not expressions — they appear directly in
the component body. Static text can be written as direct double-quoted text
children. Dynamic values and JavaScript string/template expressions still live
in expression containers `{...}`.

```tsrx
component Greeting() {
  <h1>"Hello World"</h1>
  <p>"Welcome to TSRX."</p>
}
```

```tsrx
// ❌ WRONG — bare unquoted text is not allowed
<div>Hello World</div>

// ✅ CORRECT — static text may be a double-quoted child
<div>"Hello World"</div>
<div>"Use &quot; for a quote and &amp; for an ampersand"</div>

// ✅ CORRECT — JS expressions still use braces
<div>{'Hello World'}</div>
<div>{`Count: ${count}`}</div>
<div>{user.name}</div>
```

**Why:** unquoted text is ambiguous with JavaScript identifiers. A direct text
child must be a double-quoted static string; all other values stay explicit with
`{}`.

Direct double-quoted text children use the same character-reference behavior as
quoted JSX attribute values. Use entities such as `&quot;` and `&amp;` for literal
characters that need escaping; backslashes are literal text and do not escape the
closing quote.

### Using `<>`, `<tsx>`, or `<tsrx>` for expression values

When you need UI in **expression position** — assigning it to a variable,
passing it as a prop, returning from a helper — wrap it in an expression island.
Use `<tsrx>...</tsrx>` for native TSRX template syntax. Use `<>...</>` or the
equivalent `<tsx>...</tsx>` form for JSX-style children.

```tsrx
component App() {
  const title = <tsrx><span class="title">"Settings"</span></tsrx>;
  <Card {title} />
}
```

```tsrx
// ✅ CORRECT — return native TSRX from a helper
function badge(label: string) {
  return <tsrx>
    const normalized = label.trim();
    <span class="badge">{normalized}</span>
  </tsrx>;
}

// ✅ CORRECT — pass JSX-style values via fragment shorthand
<Card
  title={<><span>Settings</span></>}
  children={<><p>Body</p></>}
/>
```

**LLM DO:**
- Use `<tsrx>...</tsrx>` when you want normal TSRX template syntax in expression position, including double-quoted text children, setup statements, and template control flow.
- Use `<>...</>` as the default wrapper when JSX-style children must live in expression position.
- Use `<tsx>...</tsx>` when you want the explicit long form of the JSX-style wrapper; it is equivalent to `<>...</>`.

**LLM DON'T:**
- Do not write `const el = <div />;` at the top level of a component.
- Do not return bare UI from regular functions without `<tsrx>...</tsrx>`, `<>...</>`, or `<tsx>...</tsx>`.
- Do not put direct double-quoted TSRX text children inside `<>...</>` or `<tsx>...</tsx>` when you mean native TSRX text; use `<tsrx>...</tsrx>` instead.

### Text children and expressions: `"..."`, `{ ... }`, `{text ...}`, `{html ...}`

Double-quoted static text may appear directly as a child. Every expression
container holds a single JS/TS expression — string, template literal, variable,
ternary, function call. Adjacent text and `{...}` containers concatenate:

```tsrx
component Inbox({ name, count }: { name: string; count: number }) {
  <p>"Hello, "{name}"!"</p>
  <p>{`You have ${count} unread messages`}</p>
  <p>{count > 0 ? 'Check your inbox.' : 'All caught up.'}</p>
}
```

Only double quotes have this direct-child meaning. Single-quoted strings and
template literals are JavaScript expressions and must stay inside `{}`.

#### `{text expr}` — forced escaped text (all targets)

Coerces the value to a string and escapes HTML-special characters. Reach for
it when the polymorphic `{expr}` could be ambiguous (e.g. the expression
might resolve to a JSX fragment or untrusted input).

```tsrx
component Price({ amount }: { amount: number }) {
  <span>{text amount}</span>
}
```

#### `{html expr}` — raw HTML

To render trusted raw HTML strings, use the `{html}` directive as child content:
`<article>{html source}</article>`.

Except for Ripple, `{html ...}` must be the sole child of a host element.
Ripple supports child-level `{html ...}` mixed with sibling children.

```tsrx
component Article({ markup }: { markup: string }) {
  <article>{html markup}</article>
}
```

**Security:** only use `{html ...}` with trusted / sanitized input — it is
an XSS vector by design.

### Lazy destructuring — `&{ ... }` / `&[ ... ]`

Two sigils that look like destructuring but compile each binding into a
deferred property/index lookup on the source. Reads stay reactive without
forcing `props.count` everywhere.

```tsrx
// Lazy object destructuring — typical use on component props
component UserCard(&{ name, age }: { name: string; age: number }) {
  <div>
    <h2>{name}</h2>
    <p>"Age: "{age}</p>
  </div>
}

// Lazy array destructuring — e.g. Solid's createSignal
let &[count, setCount] = createSignal(0);
<p>{count()}</p>
<button onClick={() => setCount(count() + 1)}>"+"</button>
```

- **React**: compiles to direct property access on the source object — no
  re-renders on stale snapshots.
- **Solid**: preserves per-access signal reactivity without a wrapper.
- **Vue**: preserves per-access proxy reactivity on props and other reactive objects.
- **Ripple**: preserves tracked reactivity; equivalent to `.value` reads
  under the hood.

Supports default values (`&{ x = 10 }`), rest patterns (`&{ a, ...rest }`),
`const` / `let` / `var`, and function parameters.

### Prop shorthand `{name}`

When a prop name matches the variable, use the shorthand:

```tsrx
// Instead of:
<Input value={value} onChange={onChange} />

// Use:
<Input {value} {onChange} />
```

### DOM refs — `{ref expr}`, `ref={...}`, and named ref props

Use refs when you need the DOM node. TSRX has three forms:

- **Keyword form `{ref expr}`** — a direct ref on the current element or component.
- **Native-style attribute `ref={expr}`** — the target runtime's native ref
  attribute. At most one `ref={...}` attribute is allowed on one element.
- **Named ref prop `inputRef={ref expr}`** — a string-keyed ref prop that are
	great for component and element composition. On host elements TSRX
  applies named ref props as refs instead of emitting them as attributes.

What `expr` can be — and how it is interpreted — depends on the target:

| Target | Direct callback | Direct mutable `let` | Direct reactive/object ref | Named mutable prop |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| React | ✓ | — | ✓ `useRef()` / `createRef()` | ✓ |
| Preact | ✓ | — | ✓ `useRef()` / `createRef()` | ✓ |
| Solid | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ native Solid refs, including arrays | ✓ |
| Vue | ✓ | — | ✓ Vue `ref()` / function refs | ✓ |
| Ripple | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ `Tracked` from `track()` | ✓ |


```tsrx
// Callback ref — every target.
<input {ref (node) => node?.focus()} />
```

```tsrx
// Mutable-variable direct refs — Solid and Ripple host elements.
component AutoFocus() {
  let input: HTMLInputElement | undefined;
  <input ref={input} {ref input} myOtherRef={ref input} type="text" />
}
```

```tsrx
// Named mutable ref prop — every target.

component Form() {
  let input: HTMLInputElement | undefined;
  <Field inputRef={ref input} />
}

component Field(props) {
  <input {...props} />
}
```

```tsrx
// React/Preact ref object + anonymous and named callback refs.
import { useRef } from 'react';

component SearchBox() {
  const inputRef = useRef<HTMLInputElement>(null);
  function logNode(node: HTMLInputElement | null) { console.log(node); }

  <input ref={inputRef} {ref logNode} logRef={ref logNode} type="text" />
}
```

**Combining refs**: you may combine one `ref={...}` with any number of
anonymous or named `{ref ...}` expressions on one host element. React,
Preact, and Vue use the target `ref` helper (`@tsrx/<target>/ref`) to merge
callbacks and ref objects. Solid emits a native ref array such as
`ref={[a, b]}`. Ripple applies refs through its block runtime.

**Component refs and Vue**: in React, Preact, Solid, and Ripple, ref props can
cross component boundaries when the child forwards or spreads props onto a
host element. Vue is special: `ref={...}` on a Vue component remains Vue's
native component ref and is not forwarded to the child's DOM element. Use a
named ref prop such as `inputRef={ref input}` when a Vue component should
expose an inner DOM ref.

**Runtime inspection**: named ref props are marked at runtime. Use
`isRefProp(value)` to detect a named ref prop. Anonymous `{ref ...}` props are
not publicly inspectable because their prop keys are unique symbols.

### Lexical template scoping

Every nested element body is its own lexical scope. You can declare
variables, call functions, and run any statements inside an element's
children — they are scoped to that block and do not leak outward.

```tsrx
component App() {
  const name = 'World';

  <div>
    // New scope — locals here are scoped to the <div>
    const greeting = `Hello, ${name}!`;
    <h1>{greeting}</h1>
  </div>
}
```

Applies to element children, component children, and every control-flow
branch (`if`, `for`, `switch`, `try`).

Plain statements (declarations, `console.log`, `debugger`) mixed with JSX
children are fine — the compiler wraps them so they execute at render, and
their locals stay scoped to the enclosing block.

### Children

Composite components accept children as nested JSX. When you need to pass a
computed value (array, function), use the `children` prop explicitly.

```tsrx
// Nested children — works as JSX statements
<Card>
  <h2>"Title"</h2>
  <p>"Content goes here."</p>
</Card>

// Passing a computed value — use the children prop
<List children={items.map(renderItem)} />
```

## Control Flow

All control-flow constructs are **real** JS statements inside templates —
not helper components.

### `if` / `else if` / `else`

```tsrx
component StatusBadge({ status }: { status: string }) {
  <div>
    if (status === 'active') {
      <span class="badge active">"Online"</span>
    } else if (status === 'idle') {
      <span class="badge idle">"Away"</span>
    } else {
      <span class="badge">"Offline"</span>
    }
  </div>
}
```

### `for ... of` with `index` and `key`

TSRX extends `for ... of` with optional `index i` and `key expr` clauses.
Use `continue` to skip an item before rendering it.

```tsrx
component TodoList({ items }: { items: Todo[] }) {
  <ul>
    for (const item of items; index i; key item.id) {
      if (item.hidden) continue;
      <li>{i + 1}". "{item.text}</li>
    }
  </ul>
}
```

- `index name` — binds the iteration index.
- `key expr` — stable identity for reconciliation. On Solid this lowers to
  `<For keyed={(item) => expr}>`. On React this becomes the `key` prop.
  On Ripple it drives the runtime's keyed for-block.
- `continue` — skips the current rendered iteration. This is the only
  supported top-level loop control-flow statement inside a component
  `for...of` body.

Top-level `return` and `break` are not allowed inside component `for...of`
loops. Use `continue` for early item skips. Use a bare `return;` only for
non-loop guard exits in the component body, and use `break` only for `switch`
cases.

Component rendering supports `for...of` for lists. Regular `for` loops,
`for...in`, `while`, and `do...while` are not supported in component template
scope. Move imperative loops into a nested function, event handler, effect, or
helper, where normal JavaScript control flow rules apply.

### `switch`

Multi-branch rendering via a standard `switch`. `break` terminates a case;
fall-through works.

```tsrx
component StatusMessage({ status }: { status: string }) {
  switch (status) {
    case 'loading':
      <p>"Loading..."</p>
      break;
    case 'success':
      <p class="success">"Done!"</p>
      break;
    default:
      <p>"Unknown status."</p>
  }
}
```

### `try` / `catch` / `pending` (error + async boundaries)

Wrap a component tree in `try { } catch (e) { }` to render fallback UI when
a child throws. Add `pending { }` to handle async (see below). All three
clauses are optional and composable.

```tsrx
component SafeProfile({ userId }: { userId: string }) {
  try {
    <UserProfile id={userId} />
  } catch (error) {
    <div class="error">
      <p>"Something went wrong."</p>
    </div>
  }
}
```

### Async boundaries

Async work is expressed with the target's own suspension primitive and wrapped
with `try` / `pending` / `catch`.

- **React + Preact**: use `lazy()` children, React/Preact suspense primitives,
  or React's `use()` style APIs where available. Top-level component-body
  `await` is supported and TSRX emits an async component function.
- **Solid**: use `lazy()` or a Solid resource that suspends under `<Loading>`.
  Component bodies are synchronous; top-level component-body `await` is a
  compile-time error.
- **Vue**: use suspending children such as `defineVaporAsyncComponent()`.
  TSRX lowers `pending` currently to Vue `<Suspense v-slots={{ default, fallback }}>`,
	until support for VaporSuspense is added by the vue team.
  Component-body `await` is not supported in Vue TSRX components.
- **Ripple**: use `trackAsync()` inside an upstream `try` / `pending`
  boundary. Component bodies are synchronous; top-level component-body `await`
  is a compile-time error.
- **React + Preact**: `for await...of` is not supported inside component
  templates. Resolve async iterables in an upstream helper and render plain data
  in the template.

```tsrx
const UserProfile = lazy(() => import('./UserProfile.tsrx'));

export component App() {
  try {
    <UserProfile id={1} />
  } pending {
    <p>"Loading..."</p>
  } catch (e) {
    <p>"Something went wrong."</p>
  }
}
```

**LLM DON'T:**
- Do not write top-level component-body `await` in Solid, Vue, or Ripple
  targets.
- Do not use `for await...of` inside React or Preact component templates.
- Do not mark components `async`.

### Early returns

Guard clauses use a **bare** `return;` (no value). Everything before the
return renders; everything after is skipped. This replaces the React-style
"return JSX early" pattern.

```tsrx
component Dashboard({ user }: { user: User | null }) {
  if (!user) {
    <p>"Please sign in."</p>
    return;
  }

  <h1>"Welcome, "{user.name}</h1>
  <p>"Here is your dashboard."</p>
}
```

**LLM DO:**
- Use `if (...) { <Fallback /> return; }` as a guard pattern.

**LLM DON'T:**
- Do not `return <JSX />;`.
- Do not `return someValue;`.
- Do not translate React-style `return (...)` directly to TSRX.

## Scoped Styles

Each component can include a `<style>` block. The compiler rewrites every
selector with a unique hash so rules only apply to elements inside that
component. Parent styles do not leak into child components.

```tsrx
component Card() {
  <div class="card">
    <h2>"Scoped title"</h2>
    <p>"Styles here won't leak out."</p>
  </div>

  <style>
    .card {
      padding: 1.5rem;
      border: 1px solid #ddd;
    }

    h2 { color: #333; }
  </style>
}
```

### Escaping scope with `:global()`

Wrap a selector in `:global(...)` to opt out of scoping — useful for global
resets or targeting child-component DOM. Works on whole selectors, multiple
selectors, or parts of a chain:

- `:global(.class-name)`
- `:global(.foo, .bar)`
- `.scoped :global(.unscoped) .also-scoped`

### Style composition via `{style}`

Because scoped classes don't cross component boundaries, TSRX provides
`{style "className"}` — an attribute-value directive that expands to a string
containing both the scope hash and the class name. Pass it to a child, which
applies it via its regular `class` attribute.

```tsrx
component Badge({ className }: { className?: string }) {
  <span class={`badge ${className ?? ''}`}>"New"</span>
  <style>
    .badge { padding: 0.25rem 0.5rem; }
  </style>
}

component App() {
  <Badge className={style "highlight"} />
  <style>
    .highlight { background: #e8f5e9; color: #2e7d32; }
  </style>
}
```

The class referenced via `{style}` must appear as a standalone selector in
the surrounding component's `<style>`. Classes that only appear as part of
compound/descendant selectors cannot be passed.

## Target-specific behavior

### React (`@tsrx/react`) — hooks + await

The compiler isolates hook calls that appear in conditional, loop, switch, try,
or expression-position `<tsrx>...</tsrx>` scopes behind generated child
components when it can do so safely. Hook results must stay local to that
generated component. Hook callbacks may read outer values, and may mutate
branch-local or module-level bindings, but mutating a parent component/function
binding across the generated component boundary is a compiler error. Move that
state into an explicit child component when values need to cross the boundary.

React additionally supports top-level component-body `await` and TSRX emits
an async component function when it appears in the component body.

```tsrx
import { useState, useEffect } from 'react';

component UserProfile({ user, posts }: { user: User | null; posts: Post[] }) {
  if (!user) {
    <p>"Please sign in."</p>
    return;
  }

  // Hook after a guard — isolated safely by the compiler
  const [tab, setTab] = useState('overview');

  <h1>{user.name}</h1>
  <TabBar value={tab} onChange={setTab} />

  // Hook inside a loop — hook results stay local to the extracted item
  <ul>
    for (const post of posts) {
      useEffect(() => {
        console.log(`viewed ${post.title}`);
      }, [post.title]);

      <li>{post.title}</li>
    }
  </ul>
}
```

### Solid (`@tsrx/solid`)

- `if` / `else` → `<Show when>` (and `<Switch>/<Match>` for if-else-if chains)
- `switch` → `<Switch>/<Match>` with `===` tests
- `for` → `<For each>`; `key expr` → `keyed={(item) => expr}`
- `try { } catch { }` → `<Errored fallback>`
- `try { } pending { }` → `<Loading fallback>`
- `<@Dyn />` dynamic elements → runtime-dispatched element
- `&{ ... }` lazy destructuring preserves Solid signal reactivity

Requires `solid-js@>=2.0.0-beta`. The `<Errored>` and `<Loading>` flow
primitives are part of Solid 2.0's public API; older Solid (1.x) is not
supported directly — use `ErrorBoundary` / `Suspense` manually if needed.

### Vue (`@tsrx/vue`)

- Compiles components to `defineVaporComponent(...)` output for the
  `vue-jsx-vapor` runtime
- Supports `if`, `for`, `switch`, `try { } catch { }`,
  `try { } pending { }`, scoped styles, and `{text ...}`
- Lowers `pending` blocks to Vue `<Suspense>` with `v-slots`; empty
  `pending {}` produces no fallback slot.  This will be replace with VaporSuspense
	once it's released.
- Supports `{ref ...}` callback refs on host elements; repeated refs are
  merged into one callback
- Use suspending children such as `defineVaporAsyncComponent()` for
  `try/pending`; component-level `await` remains unsupported
- Does not currently support composite refs, `finally` blocks, or top-level
  component-body `await`

### Ripple (`ripple`)

Ripple is both a compiler target and a runtime. See the Ripple llms.txt
(`https://www.ripple-ts.com/llms.txt`) for Ripple-specific APIs:

- `track()` / `.value` / `&[]` reactive primitives
- `effect()`, `untrack()`, `mount()`, `hydrate()`
- `RippleArray`, `RippleObject`, `RippleMap`, `RippleSet`, `RippleDate`
- `{html ...}` raw-HTML child (Ripple only)
- Vue host-element `{html ...}` lowering and callback refs
- `Context` API, `Portal`, transporting reactivity across function boundaries

## Common patterns

### Bail out of template rules with an inline function

Component bodies have template rules (statement-based JSX, no `return`
value). If you want unconstrained JS/TS logic — loops, imperative control
flow, multiple returns — put it in a plain inline function and call it from
an event handler or expression.

```tsrx
export component Counter() {
  let count = 0;

  // Plain JS — no template restrictions apply
  const increment = () => {
    if (count >= 10) {
      count = 0;
    } else {
      count += 1;
    }
  };

  <button onClick={increment}>
    {`Count: ${count}`}
  </button>
}
```

### Children via JSX vs. `children` prop

- **Nested JSX** — works for normal composition.
- **`children={expr}`** — required when children is a computed value
  (array, function, `items.map(...)`).

### Scoped locals next to the JSX that uses them

Declare `const`s mid-template so computation stays beside the markup it
feeds. Each element body is its own scope, so locals don't leak.

```tsrx
component Cart({ items }: { items: Item[] }) {
  <div>
    <h2>"Your cart"</h2>

    const subtotal = items.reduce((sum, item) => sum + item.price, 0);
    const discount = subtotal > 100 ? 0.1 : 0;

    <p>"Subtotal: $"{subtotal}</p>
    <p>"Save: $"{(subtotal * discount).toFixed(2)}</p>
  </div>
}
```

## TypeScript

`.tsrx` is a full superset of TypeScript. Props, generics, utility types,
imports — all standard TS. The compiler preserves types end-to-end and
emits framework-native TSX, so IDE tooling and `tsc` integration work as
they would with any TS project.

```tsrx
type Props<T> = {
  items: T[];
  render: (item: T) => any;
};

component List<T>({ items, render }: Props<T>) {
  <ul>
    for (const item of items) {
      <li>{render(item)}</li>
    }
  </ul>
}
```

## Quick reference

| Feature                  | Syntax                                           |
| ------------------------ | ------------------------------------------------ |
| Component declaration    | `component Name(props: T) { ... }`               |
| Exported component       | `export component Name(...) { ... }`             |
| TSRX as value            | `const v = <tsrx><span>"Hi"</span></tsrx>;`      |
| JSX as value             | `const v = <tsx><span>Hi</span></tsx>;`          |
| Text content             | `<p>"literal"</p>`, `<p>{expr}</p>`             |
| Forced escaped text      | `<span>{text expr}</span>`                       |
| Raw HTML               | `<article>{html markup}</article>`              |
| Lazy prop destructure    | `component C(&{ x, y }: Props) { ... }`          |
| Lazy array destructure   | `let &[count, set] = createSignal(0);`           |
| Prop shorthand           | `<Input {value} {onChange} />`                   |
| Callback ref             | `<input {ref (node) => ...} />`                  |
| Direct mutable ref (Solid/Ripple) | `<input ref={varName} />`                  |
| Named mutable ref prop  | `<Field inputRef={ref input} />`            |
| Reactive/object ref     | `<input ref={useRefValue} />`               |
| Multiple host refs       | `<input ref={obj} {ref a} {ref b} />`            |
| Conditional rendering    | `if (cond) { ... } else { ... }`                 |
| List rendering           | `for (const x of xs; index i; key x.id) { ... }` |
| Multi-branch             | `switch (x) { case 'a': ... break; }`            |
| Error boundary           | `try { ... } catch (e) { ... }`                  |
| Async boundary           | `try { ... } pending { ... }`                    |
| Lazy child                | `const C = lazy(() => import('./C.tsrx'));`     |
| Guard exit               | `if (!user) { <p>"signin"</p> return; }`       |
| Scoped style             | `<style> .x { ... } </style>`                    |
| Escape scoping           | `:global(.class)`                                |
| Cross-component class    | `<Child className={style "foo"} />`              |
| Dynamic element          | `<@tag />` with a `Tracked` / stateful `tag`     |

## Resources

- **Website**: https://tsrx.dev
- **Features**: https://tsrx.dev/features
- **Getting Started**: https://tsrx.dev/getting-started
- **Playground**: https://tsrx.dev/playground
- **Source**: https://github.com/Ripple-TS/ripple/tree/main/packages/tsrx
- **Ripple target (runtime APIs)**: https://www.ripple-ts.com/llms.txt

This document is optimized for AI/LLM understanding of the TSRX language.
For the most up-to-date information, visit https://tsrx.dev or the GitHub
repository.
